---
title: "Day 6: Computer Vision"
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
    center-title-slide: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: [default, ../../theme/dark.scss]
    logo: ../../theme/codercub.jpg
    highlight-style: dracula
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-gradient: "linear-gradient(135deg, #0a0e1a 0%, #0d2137 60%, #1a1f35 100%)"

---

# Introducing the Neural Network

## Introducing the Neural Network

- A computing system **inspired by the human brain**
- **1943**: Researchers publish the first mathematical model of a neuron
- **1958**: Frank Rosenblatt builds the **Perceptron**, the first trainable neural network
- **2012**: **AlexNet** wins the ImageNet competition, kicking off the deep learning boom
- **Today**: Neural networks power vision, language, and much more

## What Is a Neural Network?

- Made up of layers of connected **nodes** (neurons)
  - **Input layer**: receives raw data (numbers, pixels, text)
  - **Hidden layers**: find patterns in that data
  - **Output layer**: produces the final prediction
- Each connection has a **weight** — how much does one neuron influence the next?
- Stack enough layers and the network can learn almost anything

# Neural Network Demo

## Will I Do Well on My Test?

```{=html}
<iframe src="../components/nn.html" allowtransparency="true" width="100%" height="600px" style="border:none; background:transparent;"></iframe>
```

# Hands-On

Try out the neural network yourself

# Reflection

Did the neural network produce the results you expected?

# scikit-learn vs. Neural Networks

## scikit-learn vs. Neural Networks

- Back on Day 3, your scikit-learn model predicted **Pass / Fail** from two features: `hours_studied` and `hours_slept`
- Today's neural network just answered the **exact same question**, but works very differently under the hood

## scikit-learn vs. Neural Networks

- **scikit-learn model**: follows a fixed, human-chosen algorithm (e.g. "look at the most similar students in the data"). It tunes itself to your data, but the strategy itself doesn't change
- **Neural network**: has no built-in strategy. It *invents its own rules* by adjusting weights across layers until predictions fit the data
- Neural networks can combine features in ways no fixed formula could be written for in advance

## scikit-learn vs. Neural Networks

- With just 2 features: hours studied, hours slept — both approaches work great
- But what about **1 million features**, one for every pixel in a photo?
- A fixed algorithm can't scale to that; a neural network's layers can learn to combine pixels into edges, edges into shapes, and shapes into objects
- That's exactly how computer vision works!

# Computer Vision is Everywhere

## Computer Vision is Everywhere

![](images/autofocus.png){fig-align="center"}

## Computer Vision is Everywhere

![](images/self_driving_car.png){fig-align="center"}

## Computer Vision is Everywhere

![](images/xray.png){fig-align="center"}

# How Do Computers See?

## How Do Computers See?

- A special type of neural network called a **Convolutional Neural Network** or a **CNN**
  - Takes in all of the pixels of an image
  - Detects edges, corners, and other shapes
  - Returns results on what it sees and where

## How Do Computers See?

- Many types of computer vision algorithms:
  - **Face Detection** (is there a face in the picture?)
  - **Object Detection** (what is in this image and where?)
  - **Pose Estimation** (where are the body parts?)
  - **Gesture Recognition** (what hand gesture is this?)
  - **Image Segmentation **(what pixel belongs to what object?)

## Face Detection

- **Question**: "Is there a face in this image, and *where* is it?"
- Detects **faces** and draws bounding boxes around each one
- Not facial recognition!
- Used in:
  - Camera autofocus algorithms
  - Security cameras detecting people in the scene
  - Counting people attending an event

# Demo

Face Detection Notebook

# Hands-On

Face Detection Notebook

# Object Detection

## Object Detection

- **Question**: "What is in this image, and *where* is it?"
- Detects **multiple objects** and draws bounding boxes around each one
- Examples:
  - Self-driving cars spotting pedestrians and vehicles in real time
  - Counting inventory in a warehouse automatically
  - Accessibility tool for people with vision impairments

# Demo

Object Detection Notebook

# Hands-On

Object Detection Notebook

## Pose Estimation

- **Question**: "Where are the body parts?"
- Detects **key landmarks** on a person — joints, hands, face points
- Examples:
  - Fitness apps checking your squat form
  - Video games that respond to your movement (no controller needed)
  - Physical therapy tools tracking patient recovery

# Demo

Pose Estimation Notebook

# Hands-On

Pose Estimation Notebook

# Fun With Poses

## Fun With Poses

- How about if didn't show the camera view?
- And instead, just plotted the body joint positions...
- In a 3D Scene?

# Demo

3D Pose Avatar Notebook

# Hands-On

Rep Counter Challenge Notebook

## Next Week

- More advanced computer vision!
- Gesture recognition and Image Segmentation
- Two other mini-apps
